title: From The Temporary To The Eternal Lessons From The Life Of The Apostle Paul
scriptureForWeek: Various passages from the teachings of the Apostle Paul on how God promises to reward our hard work, faithfulness, suffering, and perseverance.
weekTheme: Boys want immediate gratification; men want eternal satisfaction God will reward those who obey Him One of the biggest questions guys your age have is, “Is it going to be worth following Jesus?” Will it be worth missing out on some of the fun stuff other guys seem to be able to get away with? Is it worth being misunderstood and seen as a bit weird because you follow Jesus? Is the sacrifice going to pay off in the long run? These are really important questions to ask, and the answers that the Bible gives will fill you with joy. Paul gives us a great example of this. Do you remember the massive price Paul paid to be loyal to Jesus? Read through this list again and check it out: I know I sound like a madman, but I have served him far more! I have worked harder, been put in prison more often, been whipped times without number, and faced death again and again. Five different times the Jewish leaders gave me thirty-nine lashes. Three times I was beaten with rods. Once I was stoned. Three times I was shipwrecked. Once I spent a whole night and a day adrift at sea. I have traveled on many long journeys. I have faced danger from rivers and from robbers. I have faced danger from my own people, the Jews, as well as from the Gentiles. I have faced danger in the cities, in the deserts, and on the seas. And I have faced danger from men who claim to be believers but are not. I have worked hard and long, enduring many sleepless nights. I have been hungry and thirsty and have often gone without food. I have shivered in the cold, without enough clothing to keep me warm. 2 Corinthians 11:23-29

days.title: A Rewarding God
days.scripture: COLOSSIANS 3:23-24 Work willingly at whatever you do, as though you were working for the Lord rather than for people. Remember that the Lord will give you an inheritance as your reward, and that the Master you are serving is Christ.
days.reflection: C.S. Lewis (he should be familiar to you by now) wrote something really interesting about the Christian life, and it's something most people have never considered. He said: “If we consider the unblushing promises of reward and the staggering nature of the rewards promised in the Gospels, it would seem that our Lord finds our desires not too strong, but too weak. We are half-hearted creatures, fooling about with drink and sex and ambition when infinite joy is offered us.” Read that quote again and let it sink in. Sometimes our culture can paint a picture that being a follower of Jesus is about getting rid of all your desires and living a life of gloom and sadness. But C.S. Lewis notes that God wants us to be people of passion and desire. What is the motivation for this? Rewards. Here is the truth of what the Bible teaches about knowing God: And it is impossible to please God without faith. Anyone who wants to come to him must believe that God exists and that he rewards those who sincerely seek him. Hebrews 11:6 There are two things you need to know about God: 1. He is real 2. He rewards Here are some things that God promises to reward you for: Feel like praying doesn’t do anything? Don’t give up. God will reward you for that. But when you pray, go away by yourself, shut the door behind you, and pray to your Father in private. Then your Father, who sees everything, will reward you. Matthew 6:6 Are people mocking you or giving you a hard time for being a Christian? God will reward you for that. God blesses you when people mock you and persecute you and lie about you and say all sorts of evil things against you because you are my followers. Be happy about it! Be very glad! For a great reward awaits you in heaven. Matthew 5:11-12 Feeling like life is too hard and you want to quit? Don’t. God will reward you for that. So be truly glad. There is wonderful joy ahead, even though you must endure many trials for a little while. These trials will show that your faith is genuine. It is being tested as fire tests and purifies gold—though your faith is far more precious than mere gold. So when your faith remains strong through many trials, it will bring you much praise and glory and honor on the day when Jesus Christ is revealed to the whole world. 1 Peter 1:6-7 Feel like doing your chores and work is pointless? God will reward you for that. Work willingly at whatever you do, as though you were working for the Lord rather than for people. Remember that the Lord will give you an inheritance as your reward, and that the Master you are serving is Christ. Colossians 3:23-24 Feel like you want to give in to what the world teaches because it's easier to fit in? Don’t. God will reward you for faithfulness. I say this because many deceivers have gone out into the world. They deny that Jesus Christ came in a real body. Such a person is a deceiver and an antichrist. Watch out that you do not lose what we have worked so hard to achieve. Be diligent so that you receive your full reward. 2 John 1:7-8 You need to understand that when you focus on temporary things, you will get temporary rewards. But when you set your heart on eternal things, you will get eternal rewards. YOU NEED TO KEEP THIS IN MIND. Living for Jesus is worth it. Being a godly man who can resist immediate gratification will set you up for maximum joy and satisfaction and minimum regret and disappointment. Don’t settle for anything less than infinite joy. He is worth it.
questions: What stood out to you from this session? Is there anything you learned?
Which of these five rewards that are listed is most encouraging or hopeful for you? Why?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how rewards motivate their faith and life. What reward are they most looking forward to?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. So, my dear brothers and sisters, be strong and immovable. Always work enthusiastically for the Lord, for you know that nothing you do for the Lord is ever useless. 1 Corinthians 15:58 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for being a real and rewarding God. Ask Him for help in any areas that you are struggling in.

days.title: Testing And Fire
days.scripture: 1 CORINTHIANS 3:10-15 Because of God’s grace to me, I have laid the foundation like an expert builder. Now others are building on it. But whoever is building on this foundation must be very careful. For no one can lay any foundation other than the one we already have—Jesus Christ. Anyone who builds on that foundation may use a variety of materials—gold, silver, jewels, wood, hay, or straw. But on the judgment day, fire will reveal what kind of work each builder has done. The fire will show if a person’s work has any value. If the work survives, that builder will receive a reward. But if the work is burned up, the builder will suffer great loss. The builder will be saved, but like someone barely escaping through a wall of flames.
days.reflection: So, if the last session was true, and we really get rewarded for living for Jesus, how does it actually work? When we stand before Jesus, does He give us a swag bag? Is there some kind of rewards ceremony like the Olympics? Is there some kind of dinner where we all make speeches, like at a wedding? Is there a formal procession and awards like a high school graduation? The truth is, it’s a bit more intense than that. Not intense based on fear, but intense based on anticipation. The Bible talks about a couple of different judgments at the end of our lives. The first one is the great white throne judgment from Revelation 20. This one is heavy. It says, “And anyone whose name was not found recorded in the Book of Life was thrown into the lake of fire.” This is a judgment of salvation. As believers, our names are written in the Book of Life. This is God's record of His people. Though symbolic (God doesn’t need an actual book), it’s the official record of those that lived for Jesus, accepted His grace, and trusted in Him. If your name is not in the book, you reject the grace of God and rebel against Him, and you will go to hell. That’s heavy. But there is another judgment called the judgment of rewards. This isn’t to see if we get into heaven; this is about being recognized and rewarded for how we served God on earth. That’s what today’s reading is about. At the end of our lives, we will stand before Jesus and give an account for what He entrusted to us. God has promised that if we serve Him, He will reward us. This is the place that determines the rewards we actually get. Our lives will go through a fiery testing. If you have ever seen how fire works, cheap things like paper and wood get burned up, but costly things like gold and precious metals get purified and make it through the fire. This is what happens when metal workers use a fiery forge to purify the metal and make it stronger (think about those epic movies where blacksmiths are hammering away at swords, making them stronger for battle). You really need to understand two things if you want to master living as a wise and godly man. First, you can build carelessly and suffer loss. Anyone who builds on that foundation may use a variety of materials—gold, silver, jewels, wood, hay, or straw. But on the judgment day, fire will reveal what kind of work each builder has done. The fire will show if a person’s work has any value. If the work survives, that builder will receive a reward. But if the work is burned up, the builder will suffer great loss. This is going to be a sad moment. All you have done will go through a testing of fire, and it will be burned up. After living with all the promises of God’s word and His grace, you didn’t do anything with them. This is like the homes you see during wildfires. The person escapes with their life and a couple of small valuables, but all they had built is burned down. You don’t want this to be you. Don’t build a life with poor- quality materials. That’s why we keep highlighting this verse from Ephesians 5:15-17: So be careful how you live. Don’t live like fools, but like those who are wise. Make the most of every opportunity in these evil days. Don’t act thoughtlessly, but understand what the Lord wants you to do. Second, you can build wisely and be richly rewarded. Anyone who builds on that foundation may use a variety of materials—gold, silver, jewels, wood, hay, or straw. But on the judgment day, fire will reveal what kind of work each builder has done. The fire will show if a person’s work has any value. If the work survives, that builder will receive a reward. Gold, silver, and jewels are symbolic of seeking first God's kingdom and living for Him. It’s about living as a man who cares about those who don’t know Jesus, walking with God, building a relationship with Him, and using your time, energy, gifts, and resources to build the church. It's about finding your calling in the world to extend God's kingdom through your job. A life set on living for God like that will be richly rewarded. You will stand before Jesus and give an account of your life. God wants to reward you. Build wisely and build well. You will be richly rewarded.
questions: Here is a cool quote by John Wesley: “I judge all things only by the price they shall gain in eternity.”
Based on how you learned eternal rewards work, how does this impact how you make decisions about your future? How might something’s “eternal value” change what you say “yes” and “no” to now?
How does the thought of standing before Jesus and giving an account of your life make you feel? What questions do you have about this?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share some decisions they have made based on short-term vs. eternal rewards. How often do they think about this, and how has it shaped their life?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Don’t store up treasures here on earth, where moths eat them and rust destroys them, and where thieves break in and steal. Store your treasures in heaven, where moths and rust cannot destroy, and thieves do not break in and steal. Wherever your treasure is, there the desires of your heart will also be. Matthew 6:19-21 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to increase your vision for eternity. Ask Him for help in learning to value what He values.

days.scripture: 1 CORINTHIANS 4:1-5 So look at Apollos and me as mere servants of Christ who have been put in charge of explaining God’s mysteries. Now, a person who is put in charge as a manager must be faithful. As for me, it matters very little how I might be evaluated by you or by any human authority. I don’t even trust my own judgment on this point. My conscience is clear, but that doesn’t prove I’m right. It is the Lord himself who will examine me and decide. So don’t make judgments about anyone ahead of time—before the Lord returns. For he will bring our darkest secrets to light and will reveal our private motives. Then God will give to each one whatever praise is due.
days.reflection: Can you be trusted? Are you trustworthy? Are you reliable and dependable? Are you a good steward of what you have been given? These are important questions to ask yourself because the world is aching for men it can trust. So much of what you are learning here on the Primal Path is designed to help you learn to steward things well so you can be entrusted with more. It’s designed to increase your capacity for responsibility and rewards. So much of what is wrong with the world today is that men cannot be trusted with what they have been given. Men can’t be trusted with money; they waste it on things that don’t matter and neglect the poor. Men can’t be trusted with sex; they watch porn, sleep around, and see women as objects for their own pleasure. Men can’t be trusted with power; they get arrogant, power up, and beat down the people below them. One of the most important things you can do as a man is to become trustworthy. It's learning to steward what God gives you in a good and godly way. This begins now, as a guy your age dealing with your issues. If you can’t develop godly habits around sex now, why do you think you will have them when you get married? If you don’t honor God with your allowance, why do you think you will honor Him when you get a real job? If you can’t keep your word and clean your room when asked, how will your boss trust you with promotions and pay? In today’s reading, Paul says that we must become good stewards of what we have been given. That’s the key to actually being rewarded. Think about it. What if you asked your friend to watch your phone for an hour, but when you came back, he had dropped it, cracked the screen, and left it in a bathroom? Do you think you would want to give him your iPad? Do you think you would leave your laptop with him? No. But what if you gave him your phone, and he cleaned the screen, got a new case, and treated it like a baby? You would want to give him more. It’s the same way growing as a man. We are rewarded with more when we are faithful with few. In the kingdom of God, there is no such thing as small things; there are only kingdom things. That’s why people say, “How you do anything is how you do everything. If you become the kind of man who can be trusted to do your chores, clean your room, do the Primal Path, and do your homework, this will lead to much larger rewards. Your parents will trust you with a car, trust you with trips with your friends, and trust you with money to build a life. Then God can trust you with a good job, a goldy wife, resources, and influence to serve others. Rewards come from being trustworthy with what you have, here and now. If you are faithful in the small things, you will be trusted with more. This is the way to greatness and rewards. Resolve to be trustworthy here and now, and much more will come your way. So, are you committed to becoming a trustworthy young man?
questions: Can you think of an area of your life where you have been trustworthy with a responsibility you have been given? How have you seen rewards from this?
Can you think of an area of your life where you have not been trustworthy with something? What has happened as a result of this?
What is something you really want your parents to let you do? How could you show them you would be responsible and trustworthy with it?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share an area of their life they have been trustworthy in and how they have seen God bless it.

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. If you are faithful in little things, you will be faithful in large ones. But if you are dishonest in little things, you won’t be honest with greater responsibilities. And if you are untrustworthy about worldly wealth, who will trust you with the true riches of heaven? And if you are not faithful with other people’s things, why should you be trusted with things of your own? Luke 16:10-12 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you grace and help you to be a good steward of the gifts and responsibilities He has given you. Ask Him for forgiveness for any areas you have been careless in.

days.title: Finishing Well
days.scripture: 2 TIMOTHY 4:6-8 As for me, my life has already been poured out as an offering to God. The time of my death is near. I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, and I have remained faithful. And now the prize awaits me—the crown of righteousness, which the Lord, the righteous Judge, will give me on the day of his return. And the prize is not just for me but for all who eagerly look forward to his appearing.
days.reflection: “It’s not how you start the race but how you finish it that counts.” Have you heard that phrase before? It's not just a cliché; it's true. It's easy to start things. It's easy to start well. When you start things, you are excited and living on adrenaline, excitement, and joy. It’s maximum possibility with minimal pain. However, you must learn that rewards do not come at the start of the race. The victory doesn’t come at the start of a battle. They don’t give out the championship trophy at the start of the season. Yet so many men naively fall into this trap. They think they are rewarded for getting going, not for getting something done. That’s where the sadness and pain set in. Starters don’t get the rewards. Starters don’t get the trophies. Starters don’t get anything unless they finish. Don’t just be a starter. Finishing things is hard. It’s hard to keep going when you are discouraged. It’s hard to keep going when you feel alone. It’s hard to keep going when the pain sets in. It's hard to keep going when everyone else bails, and you feel like you are the only one doing the right thing. It's hard to keep your word when everyone else lies. It's hard to have integrity when everyone else compromises. It's hard to go the distance. But that’s also when the glory and rewards set in. Finishers get all the rewards. Finishers get the recognition, the victory, and the joy of accomplishment. Be a finisher. This is what Paul urges you with today. Paul is in prison at the end of his life, and he is about to have his head chopped off by a Roman Executioner for his faith in Jesus. He is going to give his life for his faith. But I want you to see his attitude and his heart. He is not an old man filled with regret. He is not bitter or angry at what life has dealt him. He is full of gratitude, confidence, joy, and anticipation. Why? Because he has fought the fight and made it to the 12th round. He ran the race and made it to the finish line. He knows what comes next. Death is not the end for him; death is the beginning. He is going to see Jesus, and something wild is in store. Jesus is going to give him a crown of righteousness, and he is going to receive rewards for his faithfulness and suffering. If you could have gone down into that dark prison cell in Rome and talked to Paul, he would urge and plead with you to remain faithful to Jesus. He would beg you to be a finisher. He would urge you not to be like Demas, who bailed on his faith because he loved the things of this world. He would tell you to set your heart on the day you meet Jesus and pay whatever price to make it to the end. You are at the start of an amazing journey into manhood. It’s really amazing how far you have come this past year. You should be proud of yourself and celebrate the journey so far. But don’t get comfortable, and don’t bail. There is so much growth and glory ahead. Resolve that you will run to the end. Resolve to not quit the journey. Jesus has a crown waiting for you at the finish line. Fight to the end. It will be here sooner than you know. Rewards are at the finish line. Keep your eyes on that.
questions: Why do you think so many guys your age struggle with finishing things? What kinds of distractions or obstacles get in people’s way?
Can you think of a time when you were given a task and finished it well? How did that sense of accomplishment feel?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share about how they have endured hardship without giving up. What have they learned from this, and how does it give them hope to move forward?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Therefore, since we are surrounded by such a huge crowd of witnesses to the life of faith, let us strip off every weight that slows us down, especially the sin that so easily trips us up. And let us run with endurance the race God has set before us. We do this by keeping our eyes on Jesus, the champion who initiates and perfects our faith. Because of the joy awaiting him, he endured the cross, disregarding its shame. Now he is seated in the place of honor beside God’s throne. Think of all the hostility he endured from sinful people; then you won’t become weary and give up. Hebrews 12:1-3 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God for help and grace to finish well. Ask Him to remove any distractions that may hold you back.

days.title: Run To Win
days.scripture: 1 CORINTHIANS 9:24-29 Don’t you realize that in a race, everyone runs, but only one person gets the prize? So run to win! All athletes are disciplined in their training. They do it to win a prize that will fade away, but we do it for an eternal prize. So, I run with purpose in every step. I am not just shadowboxing. I discipline my body like an athlete, training it to do what it should. Otherwise, I fear that after preaching to others, I myself might be disqualified.
days.reflection: I hope it’s been clear this week that following Jesus isn’t about missing out on the cool stuff in life; it’s about preparing you for the best stuff in eternity. I hope you have seen that God isn’t trying to rob you of joy; he is trying to shield you from disappointment. I hope you have seen how important it is to be intentional about the kind of life you live. You will not drift into manhood. You will not wing it into heaven. You will not accidentally wake up and become a strong and noble man. This will require discipline and focus. Think about how the Olympics work. Athletes train their whole lives to try and get to the Olympics. Then when they get to the Olympics, their lifetime of training comes down to a final race. That race may last as little as 10 seconds. Usain Bolt, the fastest man to ever live and an incredible Olympic champion once said: “I trained for 4 years for 9 seconds.” Wild. Yet, what do they get for all their effort? A medal, momentary applause, and then what? Ask yourself this: Can you name five athletes from the 1980s? I bet you can’t. Yet those men gave all they had to be the best in the world, only to be forgotten. However, Paul gives us incredible hope and perspective this week. Look at what he said in today’s reading: They do it to win a prize that will fade away, but we do it for an eternal prize. Worldly athletes do all that work, and it's over before you know it. But we are called to follow Jesus like spiritual athletes, and what we get will last FOREVER. That’s why it's important that you become a man who learns to focus on the prize. Without a vision of glory, you will give up. Without a sense that this will all be worth it, you will give in to sin and temptation. Proverbs 19:18 says, “Where there is no vision, the people perish” You must keep a vision of eternity in front of you. You must fear the fire of hell and long for the hope of heaven. You must remind yourself of the day of judgment, where you will give an account for your life and be rewarded by Jesus himself. You have to remember all the great men of God in history who are now sitting in the stands of heaven, cheering you on. Just as you read earlier this week, you have to fix your eyes on Jesus so that you don’t grow weary and lose heart. Having this kind of vision will set you apart from the confused and depressed guys your age who feel like they have no purpose and future. It will save you from striving for temporary things that don’t satisfy or last. It will give you hope beyond high school, hope beyond college, hope beyond a job, hope beyond a family, and hope beyond your legacy. It will give you eternal hope. At the end of the Narnia series of books, there is an amazing quote that recaps all we have been looking at this week. The last book in the Narnia series, The Last Battle, ends with the destruction of Narnia. The characters who have died in the real world are united in "Aslan's Country," a Narnian version of Heaven. The characters find that the "real" Narnia is more vivid and more real than the one they knew, and the adventures they had in Narnia were just the beginning of a greater adventure. "And as He spoke, He no longer looked to them like a lion; but the things that began to happen after that were so great and beautiful that I cannot write them. And for us, this is the end of all the stories, and we can most truly say that they all lived happily ever after. But for them, it was only the beginning of the real story. All their life in this world and all their adventures in Narnia had only been the cover and the title page: now, at last, they were beginning Chapter One of the Great Story which no one on earth has read, which goes on forever, in which every chapter is better than the one before." You are in an eternal story. Live like it. The world is waiting for men who have made the shift from the temporary to the eternal. The type of man you are becoming. Press on. Glory ahead.
questions: Who is your favorite athlete of all time? Why? What sort of training and discipline did they go through to become who they are? How can their training inspire you to focus on your faith?
Jocko, a navy seal, says that “Discipline equals freedom.” What do you think he may mean by that?
Ask the person you are doing this study with how discipline has helped them in their life. What role has vision played in keeping them disciplined? Ask them to give a practical example of this.

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Jesus replied, “I assure you that when the world is made new and the Son of Man sits upon his glorious throne, you who have been my followers will also sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel. And everyone who has given up houses or brothers or sisters or father or mother or children or property, for my sake, will receive a hundred times as much in return and will inherit eternal life. Matthew 19:28-29 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you become a disciplined and serious man willing to fight for what really matters.

extras: WEEK 9 MANSCHOOL TIME MACHINE MAN SCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. TIME MACHINE You are almost at the end of the first year of the Primal Path. Hard to believe. Yet there are still several years ahead. As a teenager, it can be hard to feel like you are making progress. So, this week you are going to write a record of who you are, then dig it up in a year to track the growth you have seen. Get some paper and write out an assessment of who you are currently. Start with the basics: height, weight, and age. Then your favorites: favorite foods, people, music. The goal is to capture a moment and then mark your progress. You may want to bury it with an apple tag (or something equivalent). You can also take this really seriously and bury it until the end of the Primal Path. You can record yourselves and drop an SD card in as well. The goal is to see progress over time and see how your values are moving you forward. OPTION 2. WATCH IT'S A WONDERFUL LIFE This is a classic about the power of regret and the gift of life. If your son can take it in, it will bless him. https://youtu.be/iLR3gZrU2Xo ABOUT TIME A romcom with a powerful twist. It's about making sure you live well and get life right. *Note: A few adult themes in here, so your call to discern. https://youtu.be/T7A810duHvw
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
